description: A rich, leafy soup built on a palm oil and crayfish base with generous assorted meats and fish. Two leaves — pumpkin leaf and waterleaf — cooked down into a thick, intensely savoury soup. The soup that looks simple but demands respect.

ingredients.name: pumpkin leaf (ugwu)
ingredients.note: The primary leaf. Wash thoroughly, remove tough stems, and shred finely. Fresh only — frozen loses too much texture and colour.

ingredients.name: waterleaf (gbure)
ingredients.note: The secondary leaf. Reduces dramatically when cooked — do not be alarmed. If unavailable, increase ugwu to 800g or use spinach as a substitute.

ingredients.name: palm oil
ingredients.note: Do not substitute. The palm oil is the base that carries everything.

ingredients.name: beef or assorted meat
ingredients.note: Parboil, fry, and set aside. Ponmo and beef chunks particularly good here.

ingredients.name: stockfish (okporoko)
ingredients.note: Soak 30 minutes in hot water. Debone carefully.

ingredients.name: smoked fish
ingredients.note: Debone and flake into large pieces.

ingredients.name: periwinkle (isam)
ingredients.note: Traditional addition. The briny, oceanic flavour of periwinkle transforms the soup. Clean thoroughly.

ingredients.name: fresh or frozen prawns
ingredients.note: Add in the last 5 minutes. Adds sweetness and depth.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Blend separately. Vegetable soup uses pepper for background heat, not as a primary flavour.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for parboiling meat, 1 finely chopped for the base.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: This is the highest amount in any of these soup packs — vegetable soup leans heavily on crayfish for its umami base. Do not reduce.

ingredients.name: locust beans (iru/dawadawa)
ingredients.note: Rinse briefly. Particularly important in vegetable soup — adds the fermented depth that rounds out the crayfish.

ingredients.name: meat stock
ingredients.note: From parboiling the meat. Vegetable soup uses less liquid than other soups — the leaves release significant water.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: The waterleaf releases a lot of water as it cooks — this dilutes the salt. Taste and adjust only at the very end.

steps.title: Prepare All Proteins
steps.instruction: Parboil meat with seasoning, onion, and salt. Reserve stock. Fry meat until golden. Soak and debone stockfish. Debone and flake smoked fish. Peel and devein prawns if using. Clean periwinkle if using — wash thoroughly under cold running water. Set everything aside, organised and ready. Blend scotch bonnet separately with minimal water.
steps.sensory_cue: All proteins ready and within reach. Stock dark and rich. This soup moves quickly once the leaves go in — having everything prepped is not optional.
steps.failure_risk: Vegetable soup cannot be paused mid-cook once the leaves are added. If you stop to prep ingredients while the leaves are cooking, you overcook the soup. Complete all prep before starting.

steps.title: Prepare the Leaves
steps.instruction: Wash pumpkin leaf thoroughly — 3 rinses minimum. Pick off tough stems and the thick central vein from each leaf. Stack and shred finely. Wash waterleaf thoroughly — it carries a lot of sand. Shake off excess water but do not dry it completely. Do not cut waterleaf too finely — rough pieces are fine as it collapses dramatically during cooking. Have both leaves in separate bowls, ready to go.
steps.sensory_cue: Pumpkin leaf should be bright green, finely shredded, and smell fresh and green. Waterleaf should be clean, slightly damp, and look full and leafy — remember it collapses to about a quarter of its raw volume when cooked.
steps.failure_risk: Sandy waterleaf is unacceptable in the finished soup — it ruins the texture and the experience. Wash it properly. Poorly shredded ugwu creates thick, tough strips in the soup that are hard to eat with swallow.

steps.title: Heat Palm Oil and Build the Base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a large, wide pot over medium heat until clear and orange. Add locust beans if using — fry 1 minute. Add finely chopped onion and fry until soft, 3 minutes. Add ground crayfish and stir for 1 minute — it should sizzle in the oil. Add blended scotch bonnet and fry for 3 minutes. Pour in the meat stock. Bring to a simmer. Add crushed seasoning cubes.
steps.sensory_cue: The base with crayfish and palm oil should smell intensely savoury and aromatic — the highest concentration of crayfish in any of these soups gives this base a sharper, more pungent aroma. The stock-crayfish-palm oil combination at this stage smells exactly like vegetable soup is supposed to.
steps.failure_risk: Burnt crayfish in hot oil turns acrid and bitter. Medium heat only. Add it after the onion has softened the oil temperature slightly.

steps.title: Add Meat and Fish
steps.instruction: Add fried meat, stockfish, smoked fish, and periwinkle if using to the base. Stir gently to coat. Let this simmer together for 5 minutes. Taste the base now — it should be well-seasoned and bold. The leaves will dilute the flavour slightly so the base needs to be punchy at this stage.
steps.sensory_cue: The pot should look full of well-seasoned protein sitting in a rich, dark orange base. Periwinkle in the base adds a distinctly oceanic dimension to the smell.
steps.failure_risk: If the base tastes flat at this stage, fix it now before the leaves go in — a pinch more crayfish, seasoning cube, or salt. Once the leaves are in, correcting the seasoning is much harder.

steps.title: Add Waterleaf First
steps.instruction: Increase heat to medium-high. Add the waterleaf first. Stir into the base. The waterleaf will immediately begin releasing water — a significant amount. Do not panic and add more stock. Stir continuously for 3–4 minutes as the waterleaf wilts and releases its liquid into the soup. Let the soup come back to a boil with this extra liquid.
steps.sensory_cue: The waterleaf will go from a large pile to a much smaller, darker, wilted mass quite rapidly. The soup will look more liquid temporarily — this is the waterleaf releasing its water. The colour will turn a deeper, more intense green.
steps.failure_risk: Adding both leaves at the same time overwhelms the pot and steams rather than cooks the leaves properly. Waterleaf goes in first because it releases water that the ugwu then cooks in.

steps.title: Add Ugwu and Finish
steps.instruction: Add the shredded pumpkin leaf (ugwu) to the pot. If using prawns, add them now too. Stir everything together vigorously. Cook uncovered on medium heat for 5–7 minutes, stirring every minute. The soup should reduce and thicken as the excess waterleaf liquid cooks off. You want a soup that is thick, leafy, and cohesive — not watery or soupy. Taste and add salt now. Remove from heat when the leaves are fully wilted but still vibrant green.
steps.sensory_cue: Finished vegetable soup should be thick, dark green and orange, intensely savoury, and aromatic. Lift a spoonful — it should hold together as a thick, leafy mass, not drip like a broth. The leaves should be deep green and just tender. The smell should hit you immediately — palm oil, crayfish, smoked fish, and leafy greens in a single wave.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooking the leaves past this window turns them dark, slightly slimy, and grey-green — the soup loses its vibrant colour and the leaves lose their texture. Keep to the 5–7 minute window. If the soup is still too watery after 7 minutes, increase to high heat for 2 minutes and stir constantly to drive off the excess liquid.
